content.text: React UIs are built by combining small, focused components into larger ones. This pattern — called
— is the central idea behind React's component model. Instead of one large template, you describe each piece of the UI as its own component and assemble them by nesting one inside another.
Composition scales from a pair of components to hundreds. The rules are always the same: a parent renders child components in its JSX, and data flows down via props.

attrs.alt: Component composition tree showing App → Navbar/Main/Footer → ProductList/Sidebar → Card×3 → img/h3/p, with matching JSX code on the right

content.text: You nest a component simply by using it as a JSX tag inside another component's return value.
syntax is just a function call wrapped in JSX — React turns it into
When component A renders component B, A is the
Deciding whether and how many times to render the child
Positioning the child in the layout (via CSS on the parent's wrapper)

Children know nothing about their parent. They receive props and render accordingly. This one-way relationship keeps components reusable — a child can be moved to a different parent without changing its code.
Props are the mechanism for passing data from a parent to a child. Any value that a child needs but does not own should arrive as a prop.
the rendering of each item to
stays pure: given the same props it always produces the same markup.
Real applications nest components several levels deep. Each level is responsible for one concern:
The depth here is three levels:
. Each level adds exactly one layer of structure. No component tries to do everything.
A component is a blueprint — you can render as many instances of it as you like. Each instance maintains its own state and receives its own props.
instances from one component definition. React tracks each instance independently in the virtual DOM.
When to Extract a New Component
There is no algorithmic rule, but these signals suggest extraction:
— the same JSX appears in more than one place.
— a function is hard to read at a glance (more than ~20–30 lines of JSX is a signal).
— a block of JSX has its own clearly definable responsibility.
— if you imagine adding local state or a hook later, it belongs in its own scope.
Do not extract too early. Premature extraction adds indirection without benefit. Start with inline JSX, then split when the signals above appear.
Never define a component inside another component's function body.

When a component is defined inside another function, React treats every re-render as a brand-new component type. This forces React to unmount and remount the inner tree, destroying its state and causing performance issues.
a component's opening and closing tags. This enables container-style composition:
does not know what content it will receive — it just renders it in the right slot. This makes
reusable for any kind of body content.
When a container needs more than one content slot, use ordinary named props instead of
Passing JSX as a prop value works because JSX is just a value. Any prop can hold an element, a component, or an array of either.
Hard to read, hard to reuse, state management becomes tangled
Prop drilling, hard to follow, unnecessary indirection
Readable, testable, easy to locate bugs
Most React applications settle into feature-level components (e.g.
As an app grows, each component moves to its own file and is imported where needed:
Each file has one responsibility. You can read, test, and reason about each component independently.
Parents decide whether to render a child at all, which makes composition naturally conditional:
component composes three children and applies one conditional. Neither child needs to know about the others.
React does not use class inheritance to share UI logic between components. The React team recommends composition in every scenario where you might reach for inheritance:
("a WelcomeDialog is a kind of Dialog") — pass config props instead of subclassing.
— a custom Hook (covered later), not a base class.

The advantage of composition over inheritance is flexibility. A component built from props and children can be recombined in any context. A class hierarchy locks components into a rigid tree of types.
When a wrapper component passes most props straight through to a child, the spread operator keeps the code concise:
to merge its own class, then forwards all remaining props (including
. Use spreading deliberately — forwarding everything can leak props that a child shouldn't receive.

When the number of children is dynamic, you compose by mapping over data and returning an array of elements. The
prop is mandatory and must be stable and unique within the list:
React uses keys to match list items across re-renders. Using an index as a key is acceptable only when the list never reorders or filters.
Extracting too early or too late
— wait for a real signal (reuse, size, independent concern).
— causes unnecessary unmounts and remounts, degrading performance.
— when a child needs configuration, named props communicate intent more clearly.
— passing a prop through 4+ levels of unrelated components is a signal to use Context (covered later).
React decides whether to reuse or replace a component instance based on its
and its type. Two key rules govern this:
If the same component type appears at the same position across renders, React reuses the existing instance (preserving its state) and updates props.
If the component type at a position changes, React unmounts the old instance and mounts a fresh one — state is lost.
(or vice versa) at the same tree position, starting each fresh. This is worth knowing when you depend on a child's internal state.
Render Props: Passing Behaviour Through Composition
is a function prop that a parent passes to a child, which the child calls to produce JSX. It lets a parent inject custom rendering into a reusable wrapper:
owns the fetching logic; the caller controls what to render with the data. This pattern separates the
(display). In modern React, custom hooks often replace render props, but you will encounter both.

is a function that takes a component and returns a new component with added behaviour. Although custom hooks have largely replaced HOCs, they still appear in older codebases and third-party libraries:
and adds a log side effect before delegating rendering. The original
is unchanged. Prefer custom hooks for new code; understand HOCs to read existing libraries.
Use a component as a JSX tag inside another component to nest it.
Data flows from parent to child through props.
The same component definition can produce many independent instances.
Never define a component inside another component's function body.
enables container components; named JSX props enable multiple slots.
Favour composition over inheritance for shared UI logic.
